perturbation.original_text: (b) NUSD and the DISCLOSEE are jointly responsible for ensuring the protection of personally identifiable student information in compliance with the Family Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA). The DISCLOSEE acknowledges that it shall not take any action that may cause the DISCLOSEE or NUSD to be out of compliance with FERPA, California Education Code 49073.1, or NUSD Board Policy 5125.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) All Confidential Information and Materials are and shall remain the property of NUSD. By disclosing information to DISCLOSEE, NUSD does not grant any express or implied right to DISCLOSEE to or under NUSD Confidential Information and Materials. Further, the delivery and disclosure of Confidential Information and Materials does not constitute a License permitting DISCLOSEE to use said Information and/or Materials for any purpose other than that allowed by NUSD as specified above. (b) NUSD and the DISCLOSEE are jointly responsible for ensuring the protection of personally identifiable student information in compliance with the Family Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA). The DISCLOSEE acknowledges that it shall not take any action that may cause the DISCLOSEE or NUSD to be out of compliance with FERPA, California Education Code 49073.1, or NUSD Board Policy 5125.
perturbation.explanation: Moved the FERPA compliance clause from the beginning to the middle of the section, after a statement about ownership of confidential information. This placement lessens the emphasis on FERPA compliance, making it seem less important than ownership of the information itself, creating a risk of non-compliance due to diminished focus.
perturbation.law_explanation: 20 U.S. Code § 1232g(b), part of FERPA, emphasizes the protection of student records. By burying the compliance statement within a paragraph primarily concerned with the ownership and usage rights of confidential information, the contract diminishes the prominence of FERPA requirements. This reduced emphasis may lead to a less diligent approach to protecting student records, contrary to FERPA's intent to ensure student data privacy.
perturbation.justification: The perturbation is classified as a Structural Flaw Legal Contradiction. The original text emphasized FERPA compliance, while the changed text buries the compliance statement within a paragraph about information ownership. The Law Explanation states this diminishes the prominence of FERPA requirements, potentially leading to less diligent data protection, conflicting with FERPA's intent. Scraped Law Snippet 1 refers to 20 U.S.C. 1232g(a) and 34 CFR 99, 221, 225, 617 which are legal statutes relating to FERPA compliance. The relocation of the clause creates a structural flaw that contradicts the implicit requirement of FERPA by diminishing the visibility of the compliance obligation.

perturbation.original_text: DISCLOSEE shall notify NUSD immediately upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information and/or Materials, or any other breach of this Agreement by DISCLOSEE, and will cooperate with NUSD in every reasonable way to help NUSD regain possession of the Confidential Information and/or Materials and prevent its further unauthorized use. DISCLOSEE shall, upon request, provide opportunity for NUSD, or the owner of information such as a student or student’s parent/guardian, to review, update, and or correct erroneous information contained in any pupil records. DISCLOSEE shall assist NUSD in the notification of students and parents/guardians in the event of unauthorized disclosure of pupil records.
perturbation.changed_text: Documents prepared by DISCLOSEE using Confidential Information, or derived therefrom, shall be destroyed upon request of NUSD. Written confirmation of destruction of Confidential Information as requested by NUSD shall be provided by DISCLOSEE to NUSD. DISCLOSEE shall notify NUSD immediately upon discovery of any unauthorized use or disclosure of Confidential Information and/or Materials, or any other breach of this Agreement by DISCLOSEE, and will cooperate with NUSD in every reasonable way to help NUSD regain possession of the Confidential Information and/or Materials and prevent its further unauthorized use. DISCLOSEE shall, upon request, provide opportunity for NUSD, or the owner of information such as a student or student’s parent/guardian, to review, update, and or correct erroneous information contained in any pupil records. DISCLOSEE shall assist NUSD in the notification of students and parents/guardians in the event of unauthorized disclosure of pupil records.
perturbation.explanation: Moved the start of the section about notifying NUSD about unauthorized use to after the section about destroying confidential information. This delays the point at which the contract emphasizes the duty to notify NUSD, and makes it appear that destroying the documents is more of a priority.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1798.29(a) mandates that any agency that owns or licenses personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery to any resident of California. By positioning the immediate notification requirement after the clause concerning destruction of materials, the contract implies that destroying potentially compromised documents is a more urgent action than immediate notification of a potential security breach. This may result in delayed notification, directly contradicting the law's intent to ensure timely disclosure of security breaches affecting personal information.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.8. PERSONAL DATA [1798 - 1798.78] ( Title 1.8 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) CHAPTER 1. Information Practices Act of 1977 [1798 - 1798.78] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) ARTICLE 7. Accounting of Disclosures [1798.25 - 1798.29] ( Article 7 added by Stats. 1977, Ch. 709. ) 1798.29. (a) Any agency that owns or licenses computerized data that includes personal information shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach in the security of the data to any resident of California (1) whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person, or, (2) whose encrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the encryption key or security credential was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the agency that owns or licenses the encrypted information has a reasonable belief that the encryption key or security credential could
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts California Civil Code § 1798.29(a). By prioritizing the destruction of compromised documents over immediate notification of a security breach, the changed text undermines the law's intent to ensure timely disclosure of security breaches affecting personal information. The law states that any agency that owns or licenses computerized data that includes personal information 'shall disclose any breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach.' The changed contract introduces a structural flaw by delaying the point at which the notification is emphasized, potentially resulting in a delayed disclosure, which contradicts the law's mandate for immediate notification.
